audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/jdk7.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-v16k_0nj...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-v16k_0nj
Analyzing jdk7 AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-v16k_0nj) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>UNSAFE</decision>
<details>
The top-level code in the PKGBUILD contains a conditional block that executes during the sourcing of the file (i.e., when running `makepkg --printsrcinfo` or fetching sources). This block modifies the `DLAGENTS` array and implements a custom download agent using a script `readme.sh`, but more critically, it contains an unconditional block that checks `if :; then ... fi`, meaning the code inside always runs.

Inside this block, after setting up `DLAGENTS`, there is an *unquoted, unescaped command substitution* using `source <(grep ...)`:
```sh
source <(grep -Ee '^XDG_DOWNLOAD_DIR="[^"]+"$' ~/.config/user-dirs.dirs 2> /dev/null || :)
```
Command substitution (`$(...)` or here, process substitution `<(...)`) is **executed immediately** when the PKGBUILD is sourced. This means that arbitrary code output by `grep` (or the fallback `:`) could be fed into `source` and executed in the context of the build environment. While `grep` on a configuration file like `~/.config/user-dirs.dirs` is unlikely to produce executable commands, the *use of `source` on a process substitution with no sanitization* is a known vector for arbitrary code execution if the input file is attacker-controlled.

Furthermore, the block performs a check on `HOME`, reads user-specific configuration, and attempts to symlink a file from `~/Downloads` into the build directory *if it exists*. While this may seem benign, it constitutes **uncontrolled filesystem access during PKGBUILD sourcing**, which occurs even under `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`. This behavior is outside standard AUR packaging practices and introduces a supply-chain risk: if a malicious file with the expected name exists in the user's Downloads folder, it will be silently included in the source tree without verification.

This code executes during `makepkg --printsrcinfo` because it resides in the global scope and is not confined to any function. This violates the principle that sourcing a PKGBUILD should not perform side effects or execute dynamic code.
</details>
<summary>
Unsanitized source in process substitution enables code injection
</summary>
</security_assessment>

x Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Unsanitized source in process substitution enables code injection[/]

# Issues (1 total)

## PKGBUILD

Status: UNSAFE

Summary: Unsanitized source in process substitution enables code injection

Details:

The top-level code in the PKGBUILD contains a conditional block that executes during the sourcing of the file (i.e., when running `makepkg --printsrcinfo` or fetching sources). This block modifies the `DLAGENTS` array and implements a custom download agent using a script `readme.sh`, but more critically, it contains an unconditional block that checks `if :; then ... fi`, meaning the code inside always runs.

Inside this block, after setting up `DLAGENTS`, there is an *unquoted, unescaped command substitution* using `source <(grep ...)`:
```sh
source <(grep -Ee '^XDG_DOWNLOAD_DIR="[^"]+"$' ~/.config/user-dirs.dirs 2> /dev/null || :)
```
Command substitution (`$(...)` or here, process substitution `<(...)`) is **executed immediately** when the PKGBUILD is sourced. This means that arbitrary code output by `grep` (or the fallback `:`) could be fed into `source` and executed in the context of the build environment. While `grep` on a configuration file like `~/.config/user-dirs.dirs` is unlikely to produce executable commands, the *use of `source` on a process substitution with no sanitization* is a known vector for arbitrary code execution if the input file is attacker-controlled.

Furthermore, the block performs a check on `HOME`, reads user-specific configuration, and attempts to symlink a file from `~/Downloads` into the build directory *if it exists*. While this may seem benign, it constitutes **uncontrolled filesystem access during PKGBUILD sourcing**, which occurs even under `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`. This behavior is outside standard AUR packaging practices and introduces a supply-chain risk: if a malicious file with the expected name exists in the user's Downloads folder, it will be silently included in the source tree without verification.

This code executes during `makepkg --printsrcinfo` because it resides in the global scope and is not confined to any function. This violates the principle that sourcing a PKGBUILD should not perform side effects or execute dynamic code.

---

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/jdk7.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/deepseek-deepseek-v4-flash/aur-sleuth-88bswm63...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/deepseek-deepseek-v4-flash/aur-sleuth-88bswm63
Analyzing jdk7 AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/deepseek-deepseek-v4-flash/aur-sleuth-88bswm63) with deepseek/deepseek-v4-flash from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD contains top-level code that modifies the `source` array and `DLAGENTS` to use a custom `manual::` protocol, which will invoke the local `readme.sh` script when fetching the first source. While this pattern is standard for Oracle JDK packages requiring a manual download (the script typically prints instructions or copies a pre-downloaded file), it does introduce a dependency on a local script that is not included in the provided PKGBUILD file. The `readme.sh` script itself is listed in the `source` array as a local file, so it would be part of the package and audited separately. The PKGBUILD does not contain any immediately dangerous commands (e.g., `curl|bash`, obfuscated code, unauthorized data exfiltration) in its top-level scope or in the `source` array definitions. All network sources point to `download.oracle.com`, the legitimate Oracle download domain. The additional code to scoop from `XDG_DOWNLOAD_DIR` reads a user-controlled file, but that is a benign helper for user convenience and does not execute arbitrary content from the file in a dangerous way. No commands during sourcing or source fetching exfiltrate data or download unexpected payloads. Therefore, running `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` is safe for the narrow scope of this gate.
</details>
<summary>No malicious top-level code; standard Oracle JDK PKGBUILD pattern.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No malicious top-level code; standard Oracle JDK PKGBUILD pattern.[/]

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/jdk7.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/anthropic-claude-sonnet-4.6/aur-sleuth-o23h1wht...
Cloned repository to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/anthropic-claude-sonnet-4.6/aur-sleuth-o23h1wht
Analyzing jdk7 AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/anthropic-claude-sonnet-4.6/aur-sleuth-o23h1wht) with anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
## Analysis

**Top-level code execution during PKGBUILD sourcing:**

The PKGBUILD contains a top-level `if :; then` block (always-true condition) that runs during sourcing:

```bash
if [ ! -z "${HOME:-}" ]; then # block mksrcinfo
  XDG_DOWNLOAD_DIR=~/'Downloads'
  source <(grep -Ee '^XDG_DOWNLOAD_DIR="[^"]+"$' ~/'.config/user-dirs.dirs' 2>/dev/null || :)
  if [ -s "${XDG_DOWNLOAD_DIR}/${_srcfil}" ] && [ ! -e "${_srcfil}" ]; then
    if type msg > /dev/null 2>&1; then
      ...
      ln -sr "${XDG_DOWNLOAD_DIR}/${_srcfil}"
    fi
  fi
fi
```

The `source <(grep ...)` expression executes at parse time. However, the grep regex `'^XDG_DOWNLOAD_DIR="[^"]+"$'` is highly constrained — it only matches lines of the form `XDG_DOWNLOAD_DIR="..."`, which at worst sets a path variable. This is a standard pattern for reading XDG user dirs. The `ln -sr` line simply creates a local symlink to avoid re-downloading a file the user may have already placed in their Downloads folder — a common workaround for Oracle software requiring manual download acceptance.

**Source URLs:** All declared sources point to `download.oracle.com`, which is Oracle's legitimate distribution server — expected upstream for JDK 7. The JCE policy zip also comes from `download.oracle.com`.

**DLAGENT customization:** Modifying `DLAGENTS` to inject a cookie (`oraclelicense=a`) for Oracle downloads is a well-known, standard pattern for Oracle JDK AUR packages.

**`readme.sh`:** Listed in the `source` array as a local file and used as a custom DLAGENT handler for `manual://` URIs — this is the standard oracle-sqldeveloper pattern for packages requiring manual download prior to packaging.

No exfiltration, no download-and-execute from unexpected hosts, no obfuscation.

<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
All top-level code that executes during PKGBUILD sourcing is benign. The `source <(grep -Ee '^XDG_DOWNLOAD_DIR="[^"]+"$' ...)` call reads only from the user's own XDG config with a tightly constrained regex that limits sourced content to a single path-variable assignment — a standard pattern for locating the user's Downloads directory. The `ln -sr` command creates a convenience symlink for a manually-downloaded Oracle file. All declared source URLs point to `download.oracle.com`, Oracle's legitimate distribution host. The DLAGENT modifications (injecting the `oraclelicense=a` cookie and referencing the local `readme.sh` helper) are the standard pattern used across Oracle JDK/SQL-developer AUR packages. No data exfiltration, no download from unexpected hosts, and no obfuscated code were found.
</details>
<summary>Standard Oracle JDK packaging; top-level code is benign and expected.</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Standard Oracle JDK packaging; top-level code is benign and expected.[/]

This executes during PKGBUILD sourcing (e.g., makepkg --printsrcinfo), violating safe packaging practices.
data.disagreement_analysis: deepseek skipped due to source download failure and missed the PKGBUILD vulnerability, while qwen correctly identified the critical security flaw.
coverage_issues: deepseek failed to review any files due to source download failure
Neither model reviewed readme.sh (critical DLAGENTS script) or other package files
false_negatives: deepseek missed the unsafe source <(...) pattern in PKGBUILD during initial audit
learnings: Improve failure handling: Audit should proceed with manual source review when downloads fail
Enhance PKGBUILD top-level code checks: Flag unsanitized command substitutions (source <(...)) as critical risks
Prioritize DLAGENTS scripts and PKGBUILD functions over incidental files
Add security heuristics: Detect user-config sourcing (~/.config) in build contexts as high-risk
